_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve is a graph that plots Treasury yields across different maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years. A normal upward-sloping curve means longer maturities have higher yields, reflecting expectations of economic growth and inflation. Today, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.63%, and the 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread is +0.36 points, indicating a modestly upward-sloping curve. This contrasts with the period from 2022 to 2024 when the curve was inverted (spread below zero), representing shorter-term yields exceeding longer-term yields, a historically recessionary signal.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a key recession indicator because an inverted curve (spread below zero) has preceded every modern US recession by 6 to 18 months. From 2022 to 2024, the curve was inverted, signaling heightened recession risk. Recently, the spread has re-steepened to +0.36 points, moving above zero, which often happens shortly before a recession, potentially reducing the immediacy of the signal. Consistent with this, the NY Fed's recession probability remains elevated at 31%, reflecting ongoing concerns.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve shows a modestly positive slope after a prolonged inversion, which suggests caution but not an imminent recession signal. The re-steepening of the 2s10s spread above zero, coupled with a still significant 31% recession probability, indicates that risks remain elevated but timing remains uncertain.
